sound_type: loud quee-ark -- emphatic, loud, single-note whistled call that carries 200-400m through dense brush; detection paradox: loud call, invisible bird; E=4
ecosystem: Dense mountain brush -- manzanita, ceanothus, mountain mahogany, dense understory of mixed conifer forest. Mountain slopes. The densest brush available. Mid-elevation.
season: Year-round resident with walking migration: downslope in autumn, upslope in spring. Quee-ark call year-round but peak in breeding season.
family: Odontophoridae (2nd and FINAL entry. Completes the pair 159-160.)

coordinates.description: Mountain Quail in dense brush on mountain slopes of the Cascades, Olympics, and other ranges of western Washington. Mid-elevation (1,000-6,000 ft).

taxonomy_note.description: SECOND and FINAL Odontophoridae entry. Completes the New World quail pair. Largest N.A. quail (240-290g). Most secretive. Longest head plume (two straight feathers, 6-8cm). Loud quee-ark call. Walking migration. Dense brush specialist.
taxonomy_note.family_count_in_engine: Odontophoridae: second and final entry. California Quail (159) + Mountain Quail (160). Pair complete.
taxonomy_note.name_etymology: Oreortyx from Greek 'oros' (mountain) + 'ortyx' (quail) -- mountain quail. pictus from Latin 'pictus' (painted) -- referring to the colorful plumage.
taxonomy_note.genus_note: Oreortyx -- monotypic genus. Only one species. The mountain specialist of the New World quail.
taxonomy_note.order_note: Galliformes. Eighth and final entry (6 Phasianidae + 2 Odontophoridae).
taxonomy_note.family_position: Second and final Odontophoridae. Largest quail. Most secretive. Longest plume. Completes the pair and the Galliformes arc.

song_metrics.peak_energy_band_hz: 2000-3000 Hz. The stressed 'ARK' element carries the most energy.
song_metrics.song_duration_s: Single call: 0.3-0.5 seconds. Repeated persistently but less frequently than California Quail.
song_metrics.note_structure: QUEE-ARK: Emphatic whistled call. First element (quee) slightly rising. Second element (ARK) stressed, emphatic. Sometimes transcribed as 'kyow!' or 'QUEE-ark.' Single concentrated burst.
song_metrics.note_types: Quee-ark (primary -- territorial/assembly/contact), contact clucks (close range -- covey cohesion), alarm (sharp, triggering freeze or deeper retreat).
song_metrics.phrase_structure: Single emphatic note repeated. Less persistent repetition than California Quail. Called from concealed position within dense brush.
song_metrics.timbre: Ringing, penetrating, clear. The quee-ark has a whistled quality that cuts through dense brush. Surprisingly powerful for a secretive bird.
song_metrics.amplitude: E=4. Loud. 200-400 meter carry through dense brush. Detection paradox: loud enough to be heard at distance but bird remains invisible.
song_metrics.tempo: Single calls repeated at intervals. Less rapid than California Quail's chi-CA-go sequence.
song_metrics.context: Territorial announcement. Assembly. Breeding season (spring). General contact. Year-round.
song_metrics.regional_variation: Slight variation. Washington birds at northern edge of primary range.
song_metrics.key_property: QUEE-ARK -- loud, emphatic, whistled, 200-400m carry. SECOND AND FINAL ODONTOPHORIDAE. Largest quail. Most secretive. Longest plume. Detection paradox. E=4. v1.49.300.

plumage.overall: Males: blue-gray breast, chestnut throat bordered by white, bold white bars on chestnut flanks, olive-brown back. Two straight head plumes pointing backward. Females: similar but slightly duller. Both sexes beautifully patterned.
plumage.diagnostic_mark: Two straight backward-pointing head plumes (6-8cm, longest of any quail). Bold flank pattern. Larger size than California Quail. Dense brush habitat.
plumage.flight_pattern: Burst flight when forced to flush. Very brief. Immediately into dense cover. Prefers to run through brush.

physical_description.overall: Length: 26-30 cm. Weight: 240-290 g. Largest New World quail in North America. Robust build.
physical_description.diagnostic_mark: Two straight backward plumes. Bold flank bars. Larger than California Quail. Dense brush habitat.
physical_description.field_id: Large quail in dense mountain brush -- usually heard (quee-ark!) rather than seen. Two straight backward plumes visible in brief glimpses. If you can see it clearly, it's probably a California Quail. If you can only hear it, it's probably a Mountain Quail.

nodes.title: Quee-Ark Call -- Loud From Hidden Source
nodes.description: Emphatic whistled call. 200-400m carry through dense brush. Called from concealed position. Detection paradox: loud enough to detect at distance, but bird remains invisible. Often the only evidence of presence.

nodes.title: Most Secretive Quail
nodes.description: Selects densest brush. Runs deeper rather than flushing. Rarely seen despite being common. Largest N.A. quail yet most elusive. Small coveys (5-20). The detection paradox defines the species.

nodes.title: Longest Head Plume -- Two Straight Feathers
nodes.description: Two straight feathers pointing backward from crown. 6-8 cm. Longest head plume of any quail. Contrast with California Quail's forward-curving topknot. May aid brush navigation by streamlining head profile.

nodes.title: Odontophoridae Complete (159-160)
nodes.description: Two entries. California Quail (159) + Mountain Quail (160). Comparison pair: forward vs. backward plume, chi-CA-go vs. quee-ark, visible vs. secretive, open vs. dense. Equal energy (E=4 + E=4). Galliformes arc complete: 8 entries, 153-160.

nodes.title: Walking Migration
nodes.description: One of few quail species that migrates. Walks downslope in autumn, upslope in spring. Miles through dense brush. Traditional routes passed through generations. Remarkable for a small gamebird.

cross_references.relationship: ODONTOPHORIDAE PAIR! California (159) and Mountain (160). Forward vs. backward plume. Chi-CA-go vs. quee-ark. Open vs. dense. Visible vs. secretive. E=4 + E=4.
FAMILY SEQUENCE! Final Phasianidae (158) to final Odontophoridae (160). Two families complete across three degrees. Alpine to mountain brush.
GALLIFORMES ARC! Turkey (153, E=6, largest Phasianidae) to Mountain Quail (160, E=4, largest Odontophoridae). 8 Galliformes across 8 degrees. Complete.
VISIBILITY CONTRAST! Spruce Grouse: tame, approachable (fool hen). Mountain Quail: evasive, runs into brush. Both hard to see but for opposite behavioral reasons.
